dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at 21st and Broad.
Hello, I'm Jay, an EMT with the rescue squad. What prompted you to call 911 today?
Severe shortness of breath and chest pain. It started about three days ago.
Please tell me your name and where you are right now.
My name is Mary and I'm at my home.
How old are you, and when is your birthday?
I'm 61, and my birthday is April 12th.
Mary, can we check your airway to make sure everything is open and clear?
That's fine.
Your airway is open and you're maintaining it well. Thank you.
I notice you're using more effort to breathe. Let me take a count of your respirations.
Her breathing rate is 32 and somewhat labored with some accessory muscle use.
SpO2 is currently 98% on room air.
Let's check your pulse and blood pressure now.
Blood pressure is 146 over 79.
Your pulse is 116 BPM and is strong.
Can you tell me more about the shortness of breath? Is it a typical asthma flare-up?
Yes, exactly like that. I've used my nebulizer four times today but no improvement.
How long has this been going on?
It's been three days and started all of a sudden.
You've had intubations before with asthma. What has helped most in the past?
I had 16 intubations before. BiPAP has helped me more recently.
Are you currently taking any medications?
I'm on Albuterol, Symbicort, insulin, blood pressure meds, and others like Amitiza, Creon, Cyanocobalamin, Diltiazem, Ergocalciferol, Famotidine, and Xolair.
Got it. How about other medications or over-the-counter pills?
Also Dilaudid, Gabapentin, Guaifenesin, Ipratropium, Levothyroxine, Montelukast, Nystatin, Ondansetron, and Pantoprazole.
Thank you. What conditions do you have besides asthma?
I have COPD, diabetes, CHF, hyperlipidemia, and hypothyroidism.
I see. Do you have any known allergies or specific conditions we should be aware of?
I have asthma, COPD, diabetes. My allergies include Tetracycline, Reglan, Iodine or IV Contrast Dye, and Morphine Sulfate.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the tightness in your chest?
It's about 9. It feels tight around my chest.
Does the tightness get worse with any movement or deep breathing?
No, it's a constant feeling and doesn't get worse with anything.
Mary, can you follow my voice and look at me now?
Yes, I can.
I see you're spontaneous in opening your eyes. What county are we in?
[CITY] County.
Can you move all your fingers and toes for me?
Yes, easily. I have full range of motion.
Your GCS is 15, with full eye, verbal, and motor responses. This means you are alert and oriented.
Her skin is warm, dry, and has a normal color-no signs of cyanosis.
Mary, would you mind if I check your eyes for size and reaction?
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
Can you take a deep breath for me so I can listen to your lungs?
I'm hearing some wheezing, particularly when you expel air.
She appears to have full range of motion in all extremities.
Mary, we will do a quick blood sugar check. It may feel like a slight prick.
The glucose result is 400.
May we place the ECG electrodes on you to check your heart rhythm?
The four-lead monitoring shows sinus tachycardia.
Helper, check the ETCO2 now. What do you see?
ETCO2 is 30-35mmHg.
We will place you on a cardiac monitor now. It's non-invasive.
We can give you a medication to assist with your breathing, such as terbutaline. Consent?
Yes, go ahead.
I will give you a subcutaneous injection of terbutaline in your upper arm now.
Let's recheck your breathing. Has there been any improvement?
Respiratory rate remains 32, wheezing slightly diminished but still present.
Your SpO2 is still 98% and pulse remains 116 with good strength.
We need to do a 12-lead EKG now to monitor for any cardiac implications. Consent?
Sure, do what is necessary.
The results suggest the presence of STEMI. This has been reported to the hospital.
We'll give you a shot of Dexamethasone to reduce inflammation. It will be in your left shoulder now.
Let's reassess your oxygen saturation and breathing rate again.
SpO2 is still 98%. Your respiratory rate is still 32.
Has the tightness in your chest improved at all?
It's still difficult, but the tightness might be a little better.
A second dose of terbutaline is now administered into your abdomen for further assistance.
How are you breathing now-any noticeable ease of air flow?
It's still rough, but maybe a bit more air is getting through.
Respirations: 32, wheezing slightly diminished; SpO2: 98%. Stable but no significant improvement.
I will assist you onto the stretcher now and help you out of your bed carefully.
That sounds fine.
Mary, we'll wait until the stretcher is fully ready. You can stand and remain comfortable while we get it.
She is now on the stretcher and secured with a neoprene pad and a 5-point harness in place.
Latest vital signs: 98% oxygen saturation, respiratory rate of 32, and a strong pulse of 116 BPM. She remains stable.
Mary is alert and oriented-AAOx4. No apparent neurological or behavioral changes. She has full ROM in all extremities.
Her medication history includes asthma treatments (Albuterol, Symbicort), insulin for diabetes, and COPD/heart medications (Lisinopril, Diltiazem, Atorvastatin).
She also takes Dilaudid, Gabapentin, Guaifenesin, Ipratropium, Levothyroxine, Montelukast, Nystatin, Ondansetron, and Pantoprazole.
She reported chest pain (CP) that is 9 out of 10 and feels tight. Still complaint of some shortness of breath started 3 days ago.
A four-lead and two 12-lead EKGs were obtained, indicating STEMI trends. She has already been given terbutaline and dexamethasone.
A second ST segment and heart rhythm monitor were added to detect any STEMI activity during transport.
She denies improvement in her SOB but reports some slight reduction in wheezing after the second dose of terbutaline.
Mary was assisted onto the stretcher, refused ASA due to inability to chew. Airway, breathing, and circulation (ABCs) remain unchanged.
She will be transported directly to ED18 per her request. Five-point restraint is secured, and all emergency backup is either in place or en route.
Upon arrival, you'll walk to the emergency room on your own steady gait as requested.
Vital updates are being sent in real-time with destination hospital aware of diagnosis and medications administered.
Once we arrive, you'll be directly transported to ED18. Let us know if anything changes.
Know that we're here 24/7, ready to assist you if needed in the future. Thank you for allowing us to assist you today.
